questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Best practice when stating your notice period? Which answer is best?
options: State the real contractual notice (and any negotiated reality) honestly
Claim you can join tomorrow regardless of contract
Hide notice period until offer letter day
Say 'immediate' to beat other candidates
questions.explanation: False join dates break trust and often fail background/HR checks.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: On current / expected salary discussions, strongest practice is:? Which answer is best?
options: Inflate current CTC heavily to force a higher offer
Be truthful on current pay and give a researched, defensible expectation range
Refuse all numbers forever with no alternative framing
Share colleagues' private payslips without consent
questions.explanation: Honesty plus market research beats fabrication.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: If asked why you are leaving your current role, prefer:? Which answer is best?
options: Detailed personal attacks on your manager
Saying the company is collapsing as a rumor
Forward-looking growth, role fit, and learning — without burning bridges
Listing only compensation as the sole reason every time
questions.explanation: Professional, future-focused reasons read as maturity.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Best structure for 'Tell me about yourself' in HR rounds? Which answer is best?
options: Full life autobiography from childhood
Only salary history
A joke and then silence
Concise present → past → future pitch tied to the role
questions.explanation: Present-past-future keeps it relevant and brief.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: When you have a resume gap, best practice is:? Which answer is best?
options: Explain briefly and truthfully (study, health, caregiving, job search) and pivot to readiness
Lie about employment dates
Refuse to discuss gaps
Invent freelance clients that did not exist
questions.explanation: Honest brief context beats date fraud.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: If you have multiple offers, ethical practice is:? Which answer is best?
options: Fabricate fake competing offers
Share real timelines/constraints without inventing offers
Ghost all recruiters
Accept every offer simultaneously intending to renege
questions.explanation: Real leverage is fine; fabricated offers and serial reneging damage reputation.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Background verification (BGV) risk is highest when you:? Which answer is best?
options: Align resume dates with reality
Provide accurate references
Misstate titles, dates, degrees, or employment
Disclose notice period correctly
questions.explanation: BGV catches resume fraud; integrity first.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: On 'What are your strengths?', best practice is:? Which answer is best?
options: List twenty unrelated adjectives
Say you have no strengths
Only quote your LinkedIn buzzwords
Pick 2-3 strengths with short evidence relevant to the role
questions.explanation: Evidence-backed, role-relevant strengths beat laundry lists.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: On weaknesses, strongest framing is:? Which answer is best?
options: A real improvement area plus concrete mitigation steps
A fake weakness that is actually a brag ('I work too hard')
A career-ending flaw with no plan
Attacking your previous teammates as your weakness
questions.explanation: Genuine + working on it shows self-awareness.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: If asked about relocation / hybrid policy, you should:? Which answer is best?
options: Agree to anything then reverse after joining
State constraints and preferences honestly and ask about flexibility
Ignore the question
Demand remote forever without discussion
questions.explanation: Misaligned location expectations cause early exits; clarify early.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Discussing a bond / service agreement: best practice? Which answer is best?
options: Hide it and sign a conflicting offer
Advise the new employer to ignore contracts
Disclose constraints that affect joining and seek lawful options
Claim bonds are never enforceable so they do not matter
questions.explanation: Transparency on joining constraints is required for clean offers.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: When HR asks 'Why this company?', strong answers:? Which answer is best?
options: Only 'you pay more'
Generic praise that could apply to any firm
Criticism of their competitors only
Specific product/team/mission fit backed by real research
questions.explanation: Specific research signals genuine interest.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Questions YOU ask at the end of HR rounds should preferably:? Which answer is best?
options: Role expectations, team, growth, process next steps — salary timing as appropriate
Only salary on the first hello
Personal questions about the interviewer's family
Nothing; asking questions looks weak
questions.explanation: Thoughtful role/team questions show engagement; salary timing depends on stage.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: If your expected CTC is far above their band, best practice is:? Which answer is best?
options: Ghost them after the offer
Discuss band constraints early; explore total rewards or role level honestly
Accept then renegotiate daily
Insult their compensation philosophy
questions.explanation: Early alignment saves everyone time.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: On notice buyout / early release, you should:? Which answer is best?
options: Promise early release you cannot obtain
Ignore serving any notice
State what is possible after checking with current employer; do not overpromise
Start the new job while still employed full-time secretly
questions.explanation: Only commit to joining dates you can actually meet.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Sharing reference contacts: best practice? Which answer is best?
options: List people who never worked with you
Forge recommendation letters
Give HR your own alternate email as a 'manager'
Ask permission, then share accurate managers/peers who can speak to your work
questions.explanation: Consenting, real references only.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: If asked about a past layoff / firing, prefer:? Which answer is best?
options: Brief factual context, your learning, and current readiness — without bitterness
Lie and claim a promotion
A long rant naming executives
Refuse and walk out
questions.explanation: Calm ownership and forward motion beat denial or venting.

questions.stem: Accepting an offer verbally then continuing to interview elsewhere covertly is:
options: Always required
Risky to reputation; manage timelines transparently or decide cleanly
Required by all recruiters
Fine if you never tell anyone ever
questions.explanation: Clarity and integrity around acceptances protect your brand.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: When HR asks about preferred start date, you should:? Which answer is best?
options: Pick a random aggressive date for leverage
Say yesterday
Align start date with real notice, relocation, and personal constraints
Refuse to give any date
questions.explanation: Credible start dates build trust.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Best practice if you made a resume mistake discovered mid-process? Which answer is best?
options: Hope nobody notices
Double down on the error
Blame a friend who 'wrote the resume'
Proactively correct it with HR/recruiter and provide accurate facts
questions.explanation: Self-correction beats being caught in BGV.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Negotiating salary after an offer — professional approach:? Which answer is best?
options: Thank them, share a reasoned range with market/role data, and stay collaborative
Ultimatums and insults
Ignore the offer email for weeks
Accept immediately then demand more the next day without new info
questions.explanation: Grateful, data-backed, collaborative negotiation works best.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Total compensation discussions should consider:? Which answer is best?
options: Base only, ignore everything else
Base, bonus, equity, benefits, and joining constraints holistically
Only signing bonus memes
Only free snacks
questions.explanation: Compare apples-to-apples on total rewards.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: If asked about competing process stage, honesty looks like:? Which answer is best?
options: Claiming final rounds everywhere falsely
Saying you have an exploding offer that does not exist
Accurate stage and decision timeline without fabrication
Refusing any timeline discussion
questions.explanation: Real timelines help coordination; fake urgency destroys trust.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Discussing health / personal constraints that affect work setup:? Which answer is best?
options: Overshare medical details unnecessarily
Hide constraints that will make the role impossible
Demand accommodations without any dialogue
Share only what is needed for accommodations/logistics, professionally
questions.explanation: Relevant logistics honesty; privacy still matters.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: On 'Where do you see yourself in 5 years?', prefer:? Which answer is best?
options: Growth aligned with the role's realistic path and your skills
Owning their company as CEO next year
'Nowhere near here'
Exact job titles at other companies only
questions.explanation: Credible ambition tied to the opportunity.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: If HR asks about willingness to work weekends occasionally:? Which answer is best?
options: Promise unlimited 24/7 always
Be honest about boundaries while showing flexibility for true emergencies
Refuse all after-hours forever with hostility
Say yes then never respond on-call
questions.explanation: Sustainable honesty beats empty promises.

questions.stem: Handling a counter-offer from your current employer — thoughtful practice:
options: Always accept counter-offers blindly
Accept both jobs
Revisit why you interviewed; decide on role/growth, not only cash
Use counter-offers to publicly shame your manager
questions.explanation: Counters often do not fix root reasons for leaving.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: When documents (ID, education, payslips) are requested:? Which answer is best?
options: Submit altered PDFs
Refuse all verification as invasion
Send someone else's documents
Provide authentic documents through official channels
questions.explanation: Document fraud is a hard fail.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Culture questions ('describe your ideal team') — aim for:? Which answer is best?
options: True preferences that still show adaptability and respect
Whatever you think they want, even if false
Demanding only people identical to you
Saying culture does not matter
questions.explanation: Authenticity with collaboration readiness.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: If you need more time to decide on an offer:? Which answer is best?
options: Ghost until the deadline passes
Ask for a clear extension with your decision date and reasons
Accept then revoke without notice as a habit
Leak the offer online for advice from strangers only
questions.explanation: Professional extensions are normal; silence is not.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Discussing internship vs full-time conversion expectations:? Which answer is best?
options: Guarantee conversion that was never promised
Ignore performance criteria
Clarify what is evaluated and that conversion is not automatic unless stated
Threaten to leave mid-internship for leverage weekly
questions.explanation: Clear expectations prevent disappointment.

questions.stem: On 'Are you interviewing elsewhere?' a solid approach is:
options: Detailed list of every company and confidential feedback
Claim exclusivity falsely
Lie that you are not looking while scheduling five onsites
High-level honesty that you are exploring options, without oversharing
questions.explanation: High-level transparency is enough.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Visa / work authorization questions — best practice:? Which answer is best?
options: State authorization/constraints accurately and early
Misrepresent status to get past screening
Avoid the topic until day one
Use someone else's documents
questions.explanation: Immigration honesty is non-negotiable.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: If the role scope differs from the JD in HR chat, you should:? Which answer is best?
options: Ignore mismatch and hope
Clarify responsibilities, level, and success metrics before accepting
Accept anything silently
Argue angrily in the group email
questions.explanation: Scope clarity prevents bad hires.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Talking about past compensation components (bonus, stocks):? Which answer is best?
options: Mix on-target and actuals confusingly to inflate
Round everything up aggressively
Separate base vs variable vs equity clearly and truthfully
Refuse and invent a single fake number
questions.explanation: Clear structure prevents accidental or intentional misrepresentation.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: When a recruiter asks for your timeline to resign:? Which answer is best?
options: Resign before any written offer 'to show commitment'
Resign via social media first
Never resign formally
Wait for a written offer you accept, then resign per policy
questions.explanation: Written acceptance before resignation is standard risk management.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Professional follow-up after HR rounds:? Which answer is best?
options: Polite thank-you and one clear check-in on timeline if appropriate
Daily spam calls
Demanding an offer within an hour
No response ever
questions.explanation: Courteous, sparse follow-ups are enough.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: If you disagree with a rejected feedback narrative:? Which answer is best?
options: Harass the interviewer online
Optionally ask briefly for feedback; stay respectful either way
Threaten legal action casually
Apply with a fake identity next week
questions.explanation: Grace under rejection is remembered.

questions.stem: Family / marital status questions (where inappropriate or illegal by locale):
options: Overshare and invent stories
Lie elaborately
Redirect politely to role-relevant topics; know local norms/laws
Walk out cursing
questions.explanation: Stay professional; protect privacy; follow local interview law norms.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Signing an offer: before you sign, verify:? Which answer is best?
options: Only the logo looks nice
Nothing — speed matters more than reading
Only the benefits brochure images
Role, level, pay, start date, location, and key conditions match what you agreed
questions.explanation: Read the contract; mismatches are costly.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: If two companies offer similar pay, decide using:? Which answer is best?
options: Role growth, team, scope, stability, and learning — not pay alone
Coin flip only
Whichever emailed first
Whichever has the flashiest office photos
questions.explanation: Holistic fit beats tiny pay deltas.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Communicating a declined offer professionally means:? Which answer is best?
options: No reply
Timely thanks, clear decline, brief reason optional, leave the door respectful
Accept then no-show
A long critique of their interviewers by name
questions.explanation: Clean declines preserve networks.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: On 'How soon can you join if we buy out notice?' answer with:? Which answer is best?
options: Whatever number wins the race
Tomorrow without checking
Only dates contingent on real buyout approval and written confirmation
Never; buyouts are mythical so ignore them
questions.explanation: Contingent honesty prevents broken start dates.

questions.stem: Salary history bans exist in some regions; if asked illegally/inappropriately:
options: Invent a number anyway
Share every past raise drama
End the call mid-sentence always
Redirect to expectations/market for the role when appropriate
questions.explanation: Pivot to expectations; follow local rules.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Gathering HR prep documents early helps because:? Which answer is best?
options: It speeds offer/BGV and reduces last-minute errors
It wastes time
Recruiters dislike organized candidates
You should never share IDs
questions.explanation: Ready paperwork signals seriousness.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: If your LinkedIn title differs from resume, you should:? Which answer is best?
options: Keep conflicting stories
Align public profiles with truthful, consistent representation
Make LinkedIn more exaggerated for branding
Delete LinkedIn during interviews only
questions.explanation: Consistency across artifacts matters in screening.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Discussing internal referral ethics:? Which answer is best?
options: Ask a stranger to lie that they managed you
Bribe for referrals secretly as standard advice
Use real referrers who know your work; do not fabricate relationships
Ignore referral programs always
questions.explanation: Referrals must be authentic. Give the interview-safe rationale clearly.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: When an offer is below your minimum viable number:? Which answer is best?
options: Accept out of fear then resent the job
Argue on social media
Accept three roles 'just in case'
Negotiate once clearly or decline politely if still misaligned
questions.explanation: Clear yes/no after one good-faith negotiation.

questions.stem: HR asking about team conflict style — align with behavioral best practice by:
options: Describing calm escalation, listening, and issue-focused resolution
Saying you avoid all conflict permanently
Boasting you always win arguments
Refusing soft questions
questions.explanation: Same maturity bar as behavioral rounds.

questions.stem: In an interview setting, consider this question: Overall HR interview north star:? Pick the strongest answer.
options: Win by any means including deception
Accuracy, professionalism, and mutual fit — sustainable trust
Maximize offer then ghost
Minimize preparation
questions.explanation: Trust and fit beat short-term tricks.
